item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like the tie in with the Hawaii false alert. I’m a sucker for explaining weird modern phenomena, especially when it’s done as just a footnote.

On the other hand, I don’t really like the lack of narrative tension generated by a SCP that automatically neutralises all risk of nukes ever at no cost.

If I just look at it as a cute and quirky thing that does stuff, rather than a narrative, the concept of devices causing reality to correspond with lyrics of songs has been done before, and with more depth.

I’m gonna no-vote, since there’s elements I really like.

item.title: Re: Thaumiel?
item.content:encoded: Consider it to be like SCP-2000: It's Thaumiel because it keeps civilization breathing.

item.title: Thaumiel?
item.content:encoded: I like this, but I'm not entirely sure Thaumiel would be the right classification. Bearing in mind that each site has one or more thermonuclear devices buried underneath to be used in the case of an extreme containment breach, I feel like an SCP like this might be better classified as whatever the exact opposite of Thaumiel is, since it hinders the foundation's failsaves?

item.title: Really like it
item.content:encoded:
I'm a big fan of skips that clearly weren't designed by sociopaths. I like the idea that there are makers of anomalous objects that are looking out for humanity.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is pretty great work. There's some formatting stuff (maybe the list of songs and the accounts of the skip being active could be listed in a table?) but other than that it's very straight forward and to the point.

More importantly it's anomalous but also seems to be beneficial to humanity, which is rare in the Foundation. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Really good work. It is nice little piece and works well as a thaumiel despite the seeming lack of relation to anomalies. Good reference to the GOC and I like how it was specified that it was a rogue rather than depicting the GOC as trigger happy. Also wanted to ask how Sing, Sing, Sing is connected to the rest of them, what makes it special aside from the lack of references to explosions.

item.title: Re: One look and I yelled "Timber!"
item.content:encoded: @Ihp, I Dare you to update this article to include an explosion-related song 10 years from now.

item.title: One look and I yelled "Timber!"
item.content:encoded: I banged this out in maybe two hours. It's not my best work, but dammit, I've had an awful week, and writing about a ritual that says 'fuck you' to nuclear war made me feel good. What do you want from me?
